steps.title: Map the structure across the blank
steps.content: Reading what sits on each side of the blank reveals three pieces: (a) an independent clause — "American writer Edwidge Danticat, who emigrated from Haiti in 1981, has won acclaim for her powerful short stories, novels, and essays"; (b) a participial phrase — "praising her lyrical yet unflinching depictions of her native country's turbulent history"; (c) a second independent clause — "writer Robert Antoni has compared Danticat to Nobel Prize-winning novelist Toni Morrison." The participial phrase logically modifies Robert Antoni (he is the one doing the praising), so it must be attached to the second clause, not the first.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule and eliminate the others
steps.content:
Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (the comma-splice rule). They must be separated by a period or semicolon, or joined by a comma plus a coordinating conjunction. The period in choice D ends the first independent clause cleanly, and "Praising…history," then functions as an introductory participial phrase modifying "writer Robert Antoni" in the new sentence.
  • Choice A — 'essays, praising': the comma attaches the participle "praising…history" to Danticat's essays, and the sentence then runs straight into the second independent clause ("writer Robert Antoni has compared…") with no boundary — a misplaced participle plus a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — 'essays and praising': "and praising" tries to add the participle to the list ("short stories, novels, and essays"), but a participle does not parallel those nouns, and again nothing separates the second independent clause that follows. ✗
  • Choice C — 'essays praising': with no punctuation, the participial phrase fuses into the first clause and slams into the second independent clause with no boundary — a fused/run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — 'essays. Praising': the period ends the first independent clause; "Praising…history," becomes an introductory participial phrase correctly modifying the subject "writer Robert Antoni" in the next sentence. ✓
